name: TitleMax Title Loans
description_short: TitleMax Title Loans is located at 2801 E Main St, Mesa, AZ 85213. Google reviewers rate the branch 4.9 stars across 708 reviews. Call +1 480-981-8240.
description_long:
TitleMax was founded September 1, 1998 in Columbus, Georgia, and has grown into one of the largest title lending chains in the United States under its parent company TMX Finance LLC, headquartered in Savannah, Georgia. Owned by Tracy Young, the company operates roughly 900 to 1,100+ stores across approximately 13 to 16 states including Arizona, Florida, Georgia, Texas, Tennessee, and Nevada. TitleMax filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in April 2009 and emerged in April 2010. It holds no CDFI, HUD-approved, or NFCC certifications — it is a for-profit, high-cost lender, not a nonprofit financial counseling resource.

TitleMax's primary product is the car title loan — a short-term secured loan using the borrower's vehicle title as collateral, with amounts up to $10,000. Motorcycle title loans follow the same structure. In Georgia, the company operates under a title pawn model that is functionally equivalent. TitleMax also offers unsecured personal loans up to $2,500. No minimum credit score is required for any title product, and no bank account is needed for in-store transactions. First payments are typically due approximately 30 days after origination. Applications can be completed in-store or online at titlemax.com, with a mobile app available on both iOS and Android.

TitleMax's primary differentiators are speed and accessibility. The company advertises funding in as little as 30 minutes for in-store applicants, and borrowers retain full use of their vehicle throughout the repayment period — a key distinction from traditional pawn lending. With no credit score threshold and no bank account requirement, TitleMax serves borrowers who are effectively locked out of conventional lending channels. Multiple locations carry an A+ BBB rating, and some Arizona locations have maintained BBB accreditation since 1998. An online account portal at account.titlemax.com and a mobile app allow borrowers to view balances, track payment history, and make payments remotely.

The most significant drawback of TitleMax is cost. Independent reviews and regulatory findings indicate APRs typically exceed 300%, with monthly fees that can reach roughly 25% of the borrowed amount — figures the company does not publish on its website. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau issued two enforcement actions against TitleMax: a $9 million penalty in 2016 for misrepresenting loan costs and illegal debt collection practices, and a $10 million penalty plus $5 million in consumer relief in 2023 for violating the Military Lending Act by making prohibited loans to active-duty servicemembers at nearly three times the 36% rate cap. TitleMax is a documented repeat CFPB offender. Borrowers who cannot repay within the initial term face escalating fees and real risk of vehicle repossession. Any consumer with access to a credit union emergency loan, CDFI program, or employer advance should exhaust those options first.

pricing.guarantee_details: No money-back guarantee — this is a lending product, not a subscription service.

tiers.name: Car Title Loan
features: No bank account required for in-store origination
Funding in as little as 30 minutes
Borrower keeps and drives vehicle throughout loan term

features: No bank account required for in-store origination
Funding in as little as 30 minutes

tiers.name: Title Pawn (Georgia Only)
features: Available exclusively at Georgia store locations
Functionally equivalent to a title loan
Borrower retains vehicle use during pawn term

pros: No minimum credit score required — accessible to borrowers with damaged or no credit history
Funding in as little as 30 minutes for in-store applicants
Borrower keeps and drives their vehicle throughout the entire loan term
No bank account required for in-store loan origination
No prepayment penalties on any loan product
A+ BBB rating at multiple locations; some Arizona locations BBB-accredited since 1998
900–1,100+ physical store locations across ~13–16 states providing in-person service
cons: APRs typically exceed 300% — CFPB found TitleMax charged active-duty servicemembers nearly 3× the 36% Military Lending Act cap
Two CFPB enforcement actions: $9M penalty in 2016 and $10M penalty plus $5M consumer relief in 2023 — classified as a repeat federal offender
Monthly fees can reach approximately 25% of the borrowed amount per billing cycle
Vehicle repossession is a real risk for borrowers who cannot repay on the initial schedule
APR and full fee structure are not publicly disclosed on the company's website
best_for: Vehicle owners with poor or no credit who hold a lien-free car or motorcycle title and need same-day cash
Borrowers who have been denied by banks, credit unions, and conventional lenders and have no other options
People facing short-term financial emergencies in one of the ~13–16 states where TitleMax operates
Consumers who need in-person lending service and do not have an active bank account
diagnosis: TitleMax is a last-resort option for vehicle owners with poor credit who have exhausted lower-cost alternatives and need immediate cash — its no-credit-check model and 30-minute funding genuinely serve borrowers shut out of the conventional lending system. However, APRs exceeding 300%, two federal enforcement actions including one for specifically targeting active-duty military families in violation of federal law, and the tangible risk of vehicle repossession make TitleMax one of the most expensive and legally controversial lenders operating in the country. Consumers should investigate credit union emergency loans, CDFI programs, and nonprofit financial counseling before turning to a title loan.
services: Car title loans (up to $10,000, secured by vehicle title)
Motorcycle title loans (up to $10,000, secured by motorcycle title)
Title pawns (Georgia store locations only, up to $10,000)
In-store loan origination with same-day funding
Mobile app for iOS and Android
Payment management and balance tracking via app or portal
In-person customer service at 900–1,100+ store locations across ~13–16 states
